question: Recite and explain: In Matthew 5:17, what did Jesus say about the Law and the Prophets, and what theological implications does this have for the relationship between the Old and New Testaments?
optionA: He came to abolish the Law; the Old Testament is obsolete
optionB: He came not to abolish but to fulfill; He is the completion and full meaning of all that was written
optionC: He came to replace the Law with grace only
optionD: He came to make the Law optional for Gentiles
explanation: Matthew 5:17 — 'Do not think that I came to destroy the Law or the Prophets. I did not come to destroy but to fulfill.' Jesus is the fulfillment of all Old Testament type, prophecy, and covenant. The Law finds its true meaning and completion in Him. This affirms the unity and continuity of Scripture across both Testaments.

question: Expound on John 1:1-3 and 1:14. What do these verses collectively teach about the identity of Jesus Christ, and how do they establish His pre-existence and incarnation?
optionA: Jesus was the first creation of God who later became human
optionB: The Word is a title for the Bible which became important in history
optionC: The Word (Logos) is eternally God, was the agent of all creation, and became flesh — establishing Jesus as both fully divine and fully human
optionD: Jesus existed only from His birth in Bethlehem
explanation: John 1:1 — 'In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God.' John 1:3 — 'All things were made through Him, and without Him nothing was made that was made.' John 1:14 — 'And the Word became flesh and dwelt among us.' Together these verses establish the eternal pre-existence of Jesus (the Logos), His role as Creator, and His incarnation — the doctrine that God became man in Jesus Christ without ceasing to be God.

question: Explain the significance of Acts 2:38 — Peter's answer on the day of Pentecost. What three things did Peter instruct, and what does each represent doctrinally?
optionA: Repent, be confirmed, and receive the Spirit — the three sacraments
optionB: Repent, be baptized in Jesus's name for the remission of sins, and you shall receive the Holy Spirit — conversion, public commitment, and empowerment
optionC: Repent, give offerings, and join the church
optionD: Believe only — the other steps are optional
explanation: Acts 2:38 — 'Repent, and let every one of you be baptized in the name of Jesus Christ for the remission of sins; and you shall receive the gift of the Holy Spirit.' The three elements: (1) Repentance — inward turning from sin to God; (2) Baptism — public declaration of faith and identification with Christ's death and resurrection; (3) Receiving the Holy Spirit — divine empowerment for the Christian life. Together they represent the fullness of the initial gospel response.

question: In Ephesians 1:3-14, Paul describes the believer's spiritual blessings in Christ. Name and briefly explain at least four of these blessings mentioned in this passage.
optionA: Physical healing, financial blessing, long life, and happiness
optionB: Chosen before creation, predestined for adoption, forgiven through Christ's blood, and sealed with the Holy Spirit as a guarantee of inheritance
optionC: Church membership, baptism, communion, and prayer
optionD: Wisdom, wealth, power, and authority over nations
explanation: Ephesians 1:3-14 lists spiritual blessings including: (1) Chosen in Him before the foundation of the world (v.4) — election; (2) Predestined for adoption as sons (v.5) — divine sonship; (3) Redemption through His blood — forgiveness of sins (v.7); (4) Made known the mystery of His will (v.9) — divine revelation; (5) Obtained an inheritance (v.11) — eternal portion; (6) Sealed with the Holy Spirit as the guarantee of our inheritance (v.13-14) — assurance and empowerment. All blessings are 'in Christ' and 'in the heavenly places.'

question: Trace the thread of covenant through at least three key figures in the Bible books in scope (e.g., from Abraham, through the Mosaic Law, to the New Covenant in Christ) and explain how each covenant advances God's redemptive plan.
optionA: Each covenant was a failed attempt that God had to replace
optionB: The covenants are unrelated — each is a fresh start with no connection
optionC: Each covenant progressively reveals and advances God's single redemptive plan culminating in Christ: promise (Abrahamic), Law as tutor (Mosaic), and fulfillment (New Covenant in Christ)
optionD: The Old Covenant and New Covenant are identical
explanation: God's covenant history flows: (1) Abrahamic Covenant (Genesis 12, 15, 17) — unconditional promise of seed, land, and blessing through which all nations would be blessed, pointing to Christ (Galatians 3:16); (2) Mosaic Covenant (Exodus 19-24) — conditional law covenant revealing God's holiness, Israel's inability to keep it, and the need for a better covenant. It was a tutor/guardian to lead to Christ (Galatians 3:24); (3) Davidic Covenant (2 Samuel 7) — promise of an eternal dynasty; fulfilled in Jesus, the Son of David; (4) New Covenant (Matthew 26:28, Luke 22:20) — established in Christ's blood, providing forgiveness, the indwelling Spirit, and direct knowledge of God for all who believe. Each covenant is not a failure but a progressive unfolding of one eternal redemptive purpose.
